texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Information on heats of absorption, -/Delta1H abs , for acid gases (mainly CO2 and H2S) in aqueous solutions of amines is of prime importance for designing unit operations of acid gas removal because it is direct relation to the steam requirements of the amine regeneration stage and its impact on the temperature dependency of the CO2 equilibrium. As the steam cost often accounts for over half the running cost of the plant, it may be desirable that the enthalpy of absorption is low and that the data are as accurate as possible. On the other hand this may negatively impact the heat required for stripping steam.
texts.text: Information on heats of absorption, -/Delta1H abs , for acid gases (mainly CO2 and H2S) in aqueous solutions of amines is of prime importance for designing unit operations of acid gas removal because it is direct relation to the steam requirements of the amine regeneration stage and its impact on the temperature dependency of the CO2 equilibrium. As the steam cost often accounts for over half the running cost of the plant, it may be desirable that the enthalpy of absorption is low and that the data are as accurate as possible. On the other hand this may negatively impact the heat required for stripping steam.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The temperature dependency of the heat of absorption is often neglected assuming a constant value both for absorber and desorber conditions (see, for example, Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). However, experimentaldatafromtheliteratureandfromthisworkshowthat the difference between the heat of absorption at 40 ◦ C (absorber conditions) and 120 ◦ C (desorber conditions) may be larger than 25-30%.
texts.text: The temperature dependency of the heat of absorption is often neglected assuming a constant value both for absorber and desorber conditions (see, for example, Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). However, experimentaldatafromtheliteratureandfromthisworkshowthat the difference between the heat of absorption at 40 ◦ C (absorber conditions) and 120 ◦ C (desorber conditions) may be larger than 25-30%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The impact of acid gas loading on the heat of absorption is also often neglected. It may be explained by the fact that nearly all experimenters until now reported integral heats of absorption data where the effect of loading may not be seen. Kim et al. (2009) presented data on these variations and Bullin et al. (2007) show that

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Heats of absorption of CO2 with different solvents were measured in this work in a commercially available reaction calorimeter CPA-122 (Chemisens AS, Sweden) as function of temperature, loading and solvent composition over the temperature range from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Studied amines include primary amines (monoethanolamine and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol), tertiary amines (Nmethyldiethanolamine and N,N-diethylethanolamine), diamines (1-(2-aminoethyl)-aminoethanol and N-methyl-1,3-propanediamine), triamine (diethylenetriamine), and cyclic amine (piperazine). Combinations of these amines and also mixtures of potassium carbonate and piperazine were tested. Experimental heats of absorption of CO2 with these systems are compared.
texts.text: Heats of absorption of CO2 with different solvents were measured in this work in a commercially available reaction calorimeter CPA-122 (Chemisens AS, Sweden) as function of temperature, loading and solvent composition over the temperature range from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Studied amines include primary amines (monoethanolamine and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol), tertiary amines (Nmethyldiethanolamine and N,N-diethylethanolamine), diamines (1-(2-aminoethyl)-aminoethanol and N-methyl-1,3-propanediamine), triamine (diethylenetriamine), and cyclic amine (piperazine). Combinations of these amines and also mixtures of potassium carbonate and piperazine were tested. Experimental heats of absorption of CO2 with these systems are compared.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A summary on related prior work is given in Table 1. Almost all articles found in the literature give experimental heats of absorption which are integral in loading, called integral heats of absorption (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Kim and Svendsen (2007) measured heat of absorption for 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and 2-(2-aminoethyl)aminoethanol (AEEA) solutions as function of CO2 loading (differential heat of absorption). Also Carson et al. (2000) reported experimentally measured differential heats of absorption for MEA, diethanolamine (DEA) and N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)solutions at 25 ◦ Cbutatverylow loadings only (below 0.067 mol-CO2/mol-amine for 30wt% MEA).
texts.text: A summary on related prior work is given in Table 1. Almost all articles found in the literature give experimental heats of absorption which are integral in loading, called integral heats of absorption (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Kim and Svendsen (2007) measured heat of absorption for 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and 2-(2-aminoethyl)aminoethanol (AEEA) solutions as function of CO2 loading (differential heat of absorption). Also Carson et al. (2000) reported experimentally measured differential heats of absorption for MEA, diethanolamine (DEA) and N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)solutions at 25 ◦ Cbutatverylow loadings only (below 0.067 mol-CO2/mol-amine for 30wt% MEA).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental data from different authors agree that the heat of absorption increases with temperature, while the absorption capacity at high temperature is much lower than at low temperature. Higher pressure results in higher absorption capacity of the solution, since more CO2 may be dissolved at higher pressures. According to Merkley et al. (1986), heat of absorption of CO2 is independent of the partial pressure of CO2 above the solvent, but depends on the MDEA concentration in the solution. However, there is a disagreement in the literature on the relationship between the heat of absorption and the amine concentration in the solution.
texts.text: Experimental data from different authors agree that the heat of absorption increases with temperature, while the absorption capacity at high temperature is much lower than at low temperature. Higher pressure results in higher absorption capacity of the solution, since more CO2 may be dissolved at higher pressures. According to Merkley et al. (1986), heat of absorption of CO2 is independent of the partial pressure of CO2 above the solvent, but depends on the MDEA concentration in the solution. However, there is a disagreement in the literature on the relationship between the heat of absorption and the amine concentration in the solution.

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All systems studied in this work and experimental temperatures and pressures are listed in Tables 2 and 3. The scheme of the experimental setup is given in Fig. 1. The reaction calorimeter, experimental procedure and uncertainties were described in details earlier (Kim and Svendsen, 2007), so only a brief description is given here.
texts.text: All systems studied in this work and experimental temperatures and pressures are listed in Tables 2 and 3. The scheme of the experimental setup is given in Fig. 1. The reaction calorimeter, experimental procedure and uncertainties were described in details earlier (Kim and Svendsen, 2007), so only a brief description is given here.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 2 List of systems studied in this work: single amine solutions.
texts.text: Table 2 List of systems studied in this work: single amine solutions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reaction calorimeter CPA-122 is a mechanically agitated stainless still reactor of 2000 cm 3 . The well-insulated jacketed reaction vessel is suitable for work under pressures up to 100 bar and temperatures from -20 to 200 ◦ C. The system continuously measures heat production rate, reactor temperature, stirring speed, system pressure, and other parameters. All operating conditions are recorded as functions of time.
texts.text: The reaction calorimeter CPA-122 is a mechanically agitated stainless still reactor of 2000 cm 3 . The well-insulated jacketed reaction vessel is suitable for work under pressures up to 100 bar and temperatures from -20 to 200 ◦ C. The system continuously measures heat production rate, reactor temperature, stirring speed, system pressure, and other parameters. All operating conditions are recorded as functions of time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amine solution (about 1.5 kg) was charged into the reactor using the feed bottle 4. The solution was heated to the set temperature. The system was considered to be at equilibrium when no change in reactor temperature (within ± 0.01 ◦ ) or pressure (within ± 0.01bar) was observed, and when no heat transfer between the reaction system and the thermostatting media took place, /Delta1T = ( T jacket in -T jacket out ) = const . After the system reached equilibrium, an amount of CO2 (about 0.3mol) monitored by the pressure difference in the CO2 storage cylinders, 2, was fed to the reactor through the mass flow controller, 3. The system was then allowed to reach a newequilibriumbeforethenextloadingsequencecouldtakeplace.
texts.text: The amine solution (about 1.5 kg) was charged into the reactor using the feed bottle 4. The solution was heated to the set temperature. The system was considered to be at equilibrium when no change in reactor temperature (within ± 0.01 ◦ ) or pressure (within ± 0.01bar) was observed, and when no heat transfer between the reaction system and the thermostatting media took place, /Delta1T = ( T jacket in -T jacket out ) = const . After the system reached equilibrium, an amount of CO2 (about 0.3mol) monitored by the pressure difference in the CO2 storage cylinders, 2, was fed to the reactor through the mass flow controller, 3. The system was then allowed to reach a newequilibriumbeforethenextloadingsequencecouldtakeplace.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theexperiments were conducted in isothermal mode, while the pressure varied from the amine plus water vapour pressure before the first loading of CO2 , to 3 (or 10) bar during the last loading (the CO2 feed pressure). The experiments were continued until no more CO2 could enter the reactor at the set CO2 feed pressure. Higher loadings could have been achieved at higher CO2 feed pressures. The amount of CO2 fed to the reactor was calculated from the pressure difference in the CO2 storage cylinders before and after each feeding of CO2 using the Peng-Robinson equation of state.
texts.text: Theexperiments were conducted in isothermal mode, while the pressure varied from the amine plus water vapour pressure before the first loading of CO2 , to 3 (or 10) bar during the last loading (the CO2 feed pressure). The experiments were continued until no more CO2 could enter the reactor at the set CO2 feed pressure. Higher loadings could have been achieved at higher CO2 feed pressures. The amount of CO2 fed to the reactor was calculated from the pressure difference in the CO2 storage cylinders before and after each feeding of CO2 using the Peng-Robinson equation of state.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Single amine solutions and mixtures of amines were studied in the industrially important range of temperatures of from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Maximum pressure in the system was set at 3bar. Experiments at 10bar were also done with some systems (see Table 2) but no effect of pressure on the heat of absorption was found in this range. Experimental procedure used in this work was verified earlier (Kim and Svendsen, 2007) by measuring heat of absorption of CO2 with 30wt% MEA solution, used as a 'base case'. Studied amines in this work include primary amines (MEA and AMP), tertiary amines (MDEA and DEEA), diamines (AEEA and MAPA), triamine (DETA) and cyclic amine (Pz). Combinations of these amines and mixtures of potassium carbonate (K2CO3) and Pz were also tested.
texts.text: Single amine solutions and mixtures of amines were studied in the industrially important range of temperatures of from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Maximum pressure in the system was set at 3bar. Experiments at 10bar were also done with some systems (see Table 2) but no effect of pressure on the heat of absorption was found in this range. Experimental procedure used in this work was verified earlier (Kim and Svendsen, 2007) by measuring heat of absorption of CO2 with 30wt% MEA solution, used as a 'base case'. Studied amines in this work include primary amines (MEA and AMP), tertiary amines (MDEA and DEEA), diamines (AEEA and MAPA), triamine (DETA) and cyclic amine (Pz). Combinations of these amines and mixtures of potassium carbonate (K2CO3) and Pz were also tested.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: HeatsofabsorptionofCO2 withsingleaminesolventsandamine blends at 40 ◦ C (absorber conditions) are compared in Fig. 2a and b with 30wt% MEA solution. It may be seen from Fig. 2a, that -/Delta1H abs depends on amine type and CO2 loading. As expected, tertiary amines,MDEAandDEEA,givethelowestheatsofabsorption.Piperazine, having two secondary aminegroups,showheatofabsorption similar to that of a secondary amine like DEA (Oscarson et al., 1989). All other single amines containing a primary amino-group in the molecule, gave heats of absorption close to that of MEA. It is obvious that absorption capacity of the single amine solvents increase with the number of amine groups in the molecule. Among all tested amines, MEA showed the lowest absorption capacity.
texts.text: HeatsofabsorptionofCO2 withsingleaminesolventsandamine blends at 40 ◦ C (absorber conditions) are compared in Fig. 2a and b with 30wt% MEA solution. It may be seen from Fig. 2a, that -/Delta1H abs depends on amine type and CO2 loading. As expected, tertiary amines,MDEAandDEEA,givethelowestheatsofabsorption.Piperazine, having two secondary aminegroups,showheatofabsorption similar to that of a secondary amine like DEA (Oscarson et al., 1989). All other single amines containing a primary amino-group in the molecule, gave heats of absorption close to that of MEA. It is obvious that absorption capacity of the single amine solvents increase with the number of amine groups in the molecule. Among all tested amines, MEA showed the lowest absorption capacity.

texts.orig: Fig. 2b shows that heats of absorption of CO2 in amine blends are strongfunctionsofloadingandcomposition.Differentialenthalpies of absorption for mixtures are very scarcely presented in the literature. Mixtures of two solvents with different heats of absorption will not always give an 'averaged' heat of absorption for the mixture as postulated by Jou et al. (1994), since the component with faster reaction rate normally will react first. As an example the heat of absorption for the 25 wt% MDEA + 15 wt% MAPA mixture is com-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: pared in Fig. 3 with the heats of absorption with 30 wt% MDEA and 8wt% MAPA. MAPA having a higher rate of reaction than MDEA, reacts first giving a high heat of absorption in the low loading range. When MDEA starts to react, the heat of absorption in the mixture goes down because the heat of absorption for MDEA is much lower. At a loading of about 0.5 mol-CO2/mol-amine most of the MAPA is reacted, so the heat of absorption is close to equal to that for single MDEA as seen in the figure. It is obvious, that -/Delta1H abs profile with loading will depend on the composition of the mixture, i.e. the ratio of amines.
texts.text: pared in Fig. 3 with the heats of absorption with 30 wt% MDEA and 8wt% MAPA. MAPA having a higher rate of reaction than MDEA, reacts first giving a high heat of absorption in the low loading range. When MDEA starts to react, the heat of absorption in the mixture goes down because the heat of absorption for MDEA is much lower. At a loading of about 0.5 mol-CO2/mol-amine most of the MAPA is reacted, so the heat of absorption is close to equal to that for single MDEA as seen in the figure. It is obvious, that -/Delta1H abs profile with loading will depend on the composition of the mixture, i.e. the ratio of amines.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixtures of potassium carbonate and piperazine showed the lowest heat of absorption among all systems studied. Heats of absorption of 6mol/kg-H2O (17.5 wt%) K + +1.2mol/kg-H2O (7.7 wt%) Pz mixture is compared in the Fig. 4 with heats of absorption for 30 wt% MDEA and MEA solutions. The -/Delta1H abs temperature dependency for this mixture seems not to be as strong as for the amines. Also the loading capacity of this mixture decreases with temperature similar to other solvents. These may give an advantage in the regeneration stage. However, precipitation occurs here

texts.label: text
texts.orig: at loadings of about 0.45 mol-CO2/mol-(K + + Pz) (Fig. 5). Hilliard (2008) found that the precipitate is a piperazine complex which is easily dissolved at higher temperatures. No further study on precipitation was done in this work.
texts.text: at loadings of about 0.45 mol-CO2/mol-(K + + Pz) (Fig. 5). Hilliard (2008) found that the precipitate is a piperazine complex which is easily dissolved at higher temperatures. No further study on precipitation was done in this work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Heats of absorption of the same solvents at 120 ◦ C (desorber conditions) are shown in Fig. 6a and b. It may be seen from Fig. 6a andbthatall systems, except the Pz-K + mixtures, gave comparable heats of absorption.
texts.text: Heats of absorption of the same solvents at 120 ◦ C (desorber conditions) are shown in Fig. 6a and b. It may be seen from Fig. 6a andbthatall systems, except the Pz-K + mixtures, gave comparable heats of absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Amongsingle amine solutions (Fig. 6a), tertiary amines gave the lowestheatsofabsorptionasexpected.Howeverinthiswork,these amines showed a very strong temperature dependency for /Delta1H abs at loadings below 0.2 mol-CO2/mol-amine. Loading capacities for MDEA and DEEA are much lower at 120 ◦ C than at 40 ◦ C which is an advantage at the solvent regeneration stage. At a total pressure of 3 bar, no further reaction between MDEA and DEEA with CO2 wasobservedatloadingsabove0.25mol-CO2/mol-amine.Atatotal pressure of 10 bar, a loading of about 0.38 mol-CO2 /mol-amine was reached with the 30 wt% MDEA solution. DETA, AMP, MAPA and Pz used as single solvents remained quite active even at high loadings. This may indicate that more heat may be necessary to reverse their reactions with CO2 in the regeneration column.

Loading capacities for MDEA and DEEA are much lower at 120 ◦ C than at 40 ◦ C which is an advantage at the solvent regeneration stage. At a total pressure of 3 bar, no further reaction between MDEA and DEEA with CO2 wasobservedatloadingsabove0.25mol-CO2/mol-amine.Atatotal pressure of 10 bar, a loading of about 0.38 mol-CO2 /mol-amine was reached with the 30 wt% MDEA solution. DETA, AMP, MAPA and Pz used as single solvents remained quite active even at high loadings. This may indicate that more heat may be necessary to reverse their reactions with CO2 in the regeneration column.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An overview over experimental heats of absorption of CO2 with the different solvents at 40 and 120 ◦ C and for loadings between 0.1 and 0.4mol-CO2/mol-amine, is presented in Fig. 7. It is obvious that it is the amine group functionality that mainly defines the value of the heat of absorption; the order being: primary>secondary>tertiary.
texts.text: An overview over experimental heats of absorption of CO2 with the different solvents at 40 and 120 ◦ C and for loadings between 0.1 and 0.4mol-CO2/mol-amine, is presented in Fig. 7. It is obvious that it is the amine group functionality that mainly defines the value of the heat of absorption; the order being: primary>secondary>tertiary.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A reaction calorimeter was used for measuring enthalpies of absorption of CO2 with different solvents over a temperatures range of from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Different from experimental data from other sources, heats of absorption measured in this work are differential both in temperature and loading. Experiments show that enthalpy of absorption depends on the solvent type, acid gas loading and temperature. Pressure and amine concentration do not significantly affect the heat of absorption within the range studied. However, in the mixture of amines, the dependency of the heat of absorption on loading is affected by the composition of the solvent.
texts.text: A reaction calorimeter was used for measuring enthalpies of absorption of CO2 with different solvents over a temperatures range of from 40 to 120 ◦ C. Different from experimental data from other sources, heats of absorption measured in this work are differential both in temperature and loading. Experiments show that enthalpy of absorption depends on the solvent type, acid gas loading and temperature. Pressure and amine concentration do not significantly affect the heat of absorption within the range studied. However, in the mixture of amines, the dependency of the heat of absorption on loading is affected by the composition of the solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Heat of absorption is directly related to the energy used for the regeneration of the solvent. However other important properties should be also taken into account when selecting a new solvent. For example, tertiary amines like MDEA and DEEA show the lowest heats of absorption at 40 ◦ C, at the same time they show the lowest absorption capacity at 120 ◦ C indicating that less

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: I. Kim, H.F. Svendsen / International Journal of Greenhouse Gas Control 5 (2011) 390-395
texts.text: I. Kim, H.F. Svendsen / International Journal of Greenhouse Gas Control 5 (2011) 390-395

texts.label: text
texts.orig: energy may be needed to reverse the chemical reaction between these amines and CO2 in the stripper. However, tertiary amines are known to have low reaction rate with CO2 (Versteeg et al., 1996). Besides, their low biodegradability make these solvents less environmentally friendly (Eide-Haugmo et al., 2009). Potassium carbonate solutions are more environmentally friendly according to the OSPAR convention (2005) but slow kinetics (Knuutila et al., 2010) and precipitation should be taken into account when using this chemical. Polyamines like MAPA, DETA, and AEEA have higher absorption capacities and absorption rates, however higher degradation (Lepaumier et al., 2009) and higher vapour pressure may result in higher amine losses. Piperazine has lower heat of absorption than MEA, low vapour pressure, high reaction rate with CO2 and high absorption capacity (Rochelle, 2009), however it is classified as a 'red' chemical (Eide-Haugmo et al., 2009) that needs to be phased out.
texts.text: energy may be needed to reverse the chemical reaction between these amines and CO2 in the stripper. However, tertiary amines are known to have low reaction rate with CO2 (Versteeg et al., 1996). Besides, their low biodegradability make these solvents less environmentally friendly (Eide-Haugmo et al., 2009). Potassium carbonate solutions are more environmentally friendly according to the OSPAR convention (2005) but slow kinetics (Knuutila et al., 2010) and precipitation should be taken into account when using this chemical. Polyamines like MAPA, DETA, and AEEA have higher absorption capacities and absorption rates, however higher degradation (Lepaumier et al., 2009) and higher vapour pressure may result in higher amine losses. Piperazine has lower heat of absorption than MEA, low vapour pressure, high reaction rate with CO2 and high absorption capacity (Rochelle, 2009), however it is classified as a 'red' chemical (Eide-Haugmo et al., 2009) that needs to be phased out.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An ideal solvent system should give the overall lowest cost of CO2 capture with the least environmental impact. No solvent system is likely to have entirely ideal properties because of inverse correlation between some properties. Because solvent properties are directly related to the process parameters there should be a set of optimal process design and operating conditions for each solvent.
texts.text: An ideal solvent system should give the overall lowest cost of CO2 capture with the least environmental impact. No solvent system is likely to have entirely ideal properties because of inverse correlation between some properties. Because solvent properties are directly related to the process parameters there should be a set of optimal process design and operating conditions for each solvent.
